_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Pediatric: Neonatal Resuscitation. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Pediatric: Neonatal Resuscitation

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Pediatric: Neonatal Resuscitation
points: Plan for resources based on number of anticipated patients (e.g., mother and newborn or multiple births)
Provide routine care to the newly born infant
Perform a neonatal assessment
Rapidly identify newly born infants requiring resuscitative efforts
Provide appropriate interventions to minimize distress in the newly born infant
Recognize the need for additional resources based on patient condition and/or environmental factors

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Newly born infants
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Documented gestational age less than 20 weeks (usually calculated by date of last menstrual period). If any doubt about accuracy of gestational age, initiate resuscitation

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: High-quality CPR: 100-120 compressions/min, 2-inch depth, allow full recoil
AED — apply as soon as available; minimize pause in compressions
BVM ventilation: 30:2 until advanced airway placed, then 1 breath/6 sec
Control external hemorrhage if traumatic mechanism
Epinephrine auto-injector if anaphylactic cause

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Supraglottic airway (LMA/King) for airway management
CPAP/BVM with PEEP if prearrest respiratory failure
Mechanical CPR device (LUCAS, AutoPulse) when available
12-lead ECG acquisition to identify reversible causes (hyperkalemia, STEMI)
IV access maintenance (existing access only)
Oral glucose if hypoglycemic cause suspected

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IO access initiation for medication and fluid delivery
Peripheral IV access initiation
Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO q 3-5 min (standard arrest dose)
IV/IO fluid resuscitation if hypovolemic cause suspected
IV D50W or dextrose for hypoglycemic cause
IV naloxone for opioid-induced cardiac arrest
Waveform capnography for CPR quality feedback (EtCO2 >20 mmHg suggests effective CPR; >40 mmHg suggests ROSC)

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Endotracheal intubation: continuous waveform capnography after placement
Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO (VF/pVT shock-refractory); 150 mg additional dose
Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg IV/IO as alternative to amiodarone
Sodium bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg IV/IO for prolonged arrest, hyperkalemia, TCA overdose
Calcium chloride 1 g IV/IO for hyperkalemia or CCB overdose
ROSC: post-ROSC care protocol — targeted temperature management, avoid hypoxia/hypotension
Transcutaneous pacing for PEA with bradycardia mechanism
Point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) to identify reversible causes (pneumothorax, tamponade)

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Hypothermia is common in newborns and worsens outcomes of nearly all post-natal complications a. Ensure heat retention by drying the infant thoroughly, covering the head, and wrapping the baby in dry cloth
During transport, neonate should be appropriately secured (e.g., secured to mother with approved neonatal restraint system, car seat or isolette) and mother should be appropriately secured

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Approximately 10% of newly born infants require some assistance to begin breathing at birth and 1% require resuscitation to support perfusion

Most newborns require only drying, warming, and stimulating to help them transition from fetal respiration to newborn respiration. The resuscitation sequence can be remembered as Dry, Warm, and Stimulate ' Ventilate ' Evaluate ' and Resuscitate
Deliveries complicated by maternal bleeding (placenta previa, vas previa, or placental abruption) place the infant at risk for hypovolemia secondary to blood loss
Low birth weight infants are at high-risk for hypothermia due to heat loss

Measuring the pulse oximetry on the right hand provides the most accurate oxygen saturation (SpO2) in infants that are transitioning from fetal to normal circulation. At 60 seconds, 60% is the target with an increase of 5% every minute until 5 minutes of life when pulse oximetry is 80'85%
Both hypoxia and excess oxygen administration can result in harm to the infant. If prolonged oxygen use is required, titrate to maintain an SPO2 of 85'95%

While not ideal, a larger facemask than indicated for patient size may be used to provide BVM ventilation if an appropriately sized mask is not available. Avoid pressure over the eyes as this may result in bradycardia
Increase in heart rate is the most reliable indicator of effective resuscitative efforts

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Historical elements o Prenatal complications
o Delivery complications
o Date and time of birth
o Estimated gestational age
' Physical exam findings o Heart rate
o Respiratory rate
o Respiratory effort
o Appearance

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
